title: 9 Tiqimt — St. Samuel the Prophet & The Assembly
narrative:
Tiqimt

St. Samuel the Prophet (Monthly Commemoration)

St. James the Son of Alphaeus, Apostle

St. Andronicus and Athanasia, Monastics

On the ninth day of Tiqimt, the monthly commemoration of the Prophet Samuel arrives with the particular counsel of the one who hears his name called in the night. Samuel's call narrative — 'Samuel, Samuel!' — is the paradigmatic story of divine address in the interior: God speaking not in a public oracle but in the vulnerable space of sleep, in the darkness of the Temple where the lamp of God had not yet gone out. Samuel did not recognize the voice at first, and required the old Eli's guidance to understand that what he was hearing was not the priest but the Lord. This detail has been instructive for the entire subsequent tradition: the divine voice is often not self-evidently divine, and the community of the wise — the spiritual director, the elder, the Church's tradition of discernment — is necessary to help the hearer understand what they are hearing. The interior life is not a solitary enterprise; it is practiced in community, under guidance.

St. James the Son of Alphaeus — James the Less, as he is sometimes called to distinguish him from the son of Zebedee — is one of the most quietly faithful of all the apostles. He appears in the lists of the Twelve without the dramatic personal narrative that attaches to Peter, John, or Thomas. Tradition assigns him a mission in Persia or Egypt; he died a martyr's death by being thrown from the pinnacle of the Temple and then beaten with a fuller's club. His very ordinariness in the apostolic company is his Tiqimt virtue: the month of interiority has no need for the dramatic, and James the Less is the apostle who demonstrates that faithfulness unto death does not require a vivid personal story to be real and to matter. He is the patron of those who are not the center of the narrative, and the covenant of mercy is equally available to them.

Sts. Andronicus and Athanasia were a married couple of Alexandria who lost their two children in a single day, grieved together, and then by mutual consent entered the monastic life separately — Andronicus at the community of Tabennisi, Athanasia at a women's monastery. Years later, Andronicus, traveling as a pilgrim, encountered a monk named Athanasios on the road without recognizing him as his wife. They traveled together, formed a monastic bond, and lived for years as fellow monks before Athanasia, on her deathbed, revealed her identity to Andronicus. The reunion in death — the recognition of the beloved face at the very threshold of eternity — is one of the tradition's most tender images of what the covenant of mercy ultimately accomplishes: nothing loved in God is permanently lost.

Samuel, James, and Andronicus and Athanasia together on the ninth day of Tiqimt present the interior life in three of its most characteristic conditions: the one who hears the divine address and needs a guide to understand it; the one who faithfully fulfills his vocation without ever becoming the narrative's center; and the two who chose the narrow path of separation and found, at the end of the path, that the one they had given up was given back in a form more complete than the one they had surrendered. The Covenant of Mercy does not forget what has been offered into its keeping.
